screen.name: photo1_1
screen.title: Body Position
screen.desc: Open the floor plan of the pain clinic to start creating the blueprint.

screen.name: photo1_2
screen.title: Body Position
screen.desc:
This is the floor plan the pain clinic provided.
Click on the victim's office to zoom in.
screen.caption: Office Dr. Fowler
Office Dr. Justine Phillips
Front desk

screen.name: photo1_3
screen.title: Body Position
screen.desc:
The position of the furniture is already placed as it was when the victim was found.
Now click and drag the drawing that represents the victim to the correct area in the floor plan.

screen.name: photo1_4
screen.title: Body Position
screen.desc: The next step is to produce a 3D-blueprint of the room. The computer is going to take care of this based on the floor plan measurements.

screen.name: photo1_6
screen.title: 3D blueprint of crime scene
screen.desc:
The image shows the position of the body as it was found at the crime scene.
Use this image later to indicate the victim’s position at the time the bullet hit her.

screen.name: photo2_1
screen.title: Bloodstains on the floor
screen.desc: Use the zoom to magnify the bloodstains.

screen.name: photo2_2
screen.title: Bloodstains on the floor
screen.desc: Open the database to analyze these bloodstains.

screen.name: photo2_3
screen.title: Bloodstains on the floor
screen.desc:
The size and shape of bloodstains depends on the way they were created.
Most bloodstains found at a crime scene are either impact stains, passive stains, or transfer stains.
screen.popup:
Impact stains (blood spatter)
Caused by an external force to the blood source
Stains less than 4 mm in width

Passive stains (drip stains)
Caused by blood dripping down due to gravity only
Stains more than 4 mm in width

Transfer stains
Caused by bloodstained objects that strike a surface
Usually mirror images of the object (here: a hand)

screen.name: photo2_4
screen.title: Bloodstains on the floor
screen.desc:
To determine the width of these stain, we’re going to use an ABFO scale.
Click on the scale to learn how to use it.

screen.name: scaleScreen
screen.desc:
Originally designed by the American Board of Forensic Odontology (ABFO), this scale is the standard for many forensic measurements.
The grid is divided into 5 centimeters (cm), each containing 10 millimeters (mm).
screen.caption: 1 mm
1 cm

screen.name: photo2_5
screen.title: Bloodstains on the floor
screen.desc: What is the width of our stains? Use the ABFO scale to measure the stains.
screen.response: That's not what I have measured. Try again.
Are you sure? The stains look bigger to me. Try again.

screen.name: photo2_6
screen.title: Bloodstains on the floor
screen.desc: Based on their width and shape, what type of stains are we looking at?
screen.response: Correct. Based on their size and shape, we can conclude that these stains are passive stains, or drip stains.
If those stains were impact stains, they would be much smaller than 4 millimeters. Try again.
I don't see a mirror image of any object. Try again.

screen.name: photo2_7
screen.title: Bloodstains on the floor
screen.desc: Based on your findings, what can you conclude so far?
screen.option: The body was placed in the area of the drip stains.
The victim was in the area of the drip stains when shot.
screen.response: We can't conclude this based on the bloodstains. Try again.
If that were the case, there would be more bloodstains in other areas of the room. Try again.
Yes! Since the blood dripped straight down from the source due to gravity, our victim must have been located in the area of the stains when she was shot.

screen.name: photo2_8
screen.desc:
The next step is to indicate the position of the victim at the time the bullet hit her in our 3D blueprint.
Click on the area of the drip stains to do that.

screen.name: photo3_1
screen.title: Bloodstains on the wall
screen.desc: First, zoom into the bloodstain pattern.

screen.name: photo3_2
screen.title: Bloodstains on the wall
screen.desc:
What is the WIDTH of these stains?
Click the ABFO scale if you want a reminder how to read it.
screen.response:
No, none of the stains are that wide.
Try again and measure the narrow parts of the stains, not the length.

I don't see any stain that is that wide.
Try again and measure the narrow parts of the stains, not the length.

screen.name: photo3_3
screen.title: Bloodstains on the wall
screen.desc: Based on the width and shape of the stains, what type of stains are they?
screen.response: These stains are way too small to be passive stains. Try again.
Yes. We’re looking at a blood spatter pattern caused by the impact of an object on the blood-shedding person.
If these were transfer stains, the pattern would resemble an object. Try again.

screen.name: photo3_4
screen.title: Bloodstains on the wall
screen.desc: When reconstructing a crime scene from impact stains, scientists look at three main characteristics of the stains:

screen.name: photo3_5
screen.title: Bloodstains on the wall: DIRECTION OF TRAVEL
screen.desc:
The physical appearance of the bloodstains can be used to determine the direction the blood traveled when it touched the wall.
The pointed end of the stain always faces its direction of travel.
screen.example: Blood traveled from left to right
Blood traveled from top to bottom
Blood traveled from right to left
screen.caption: Pointed end of stain
screen.popup: Blood spatter pattern produced by blood drops traveling from left to right.
Blood spatter pattern produced by blood drops traveling from top to bottom.
Blood spatter pattern produced by blood drops traveling from right to left.

screen.name: photo3_6
screen.title: Bloodstains on the wall: DIRECTION OF TRAVEL
screen.desc: In which direction did the blood travel?
screen.response: I agree. The pointed ends of the stains face to the right, which means the blood traveled from left to right.
If that were the case, the pointed ends of the stains would face downwards. Try again.
Remember, the pointed end of the stains always face the direction of travel. Try again.

screen.name: photo3_7
screen.title: Bloodstains on the wall: POINT OF CONVERGENCE
screen.desc: First, zoom out of the picture to see a larger area.

screen.name: photo3_8
screen.title: Bloodstains on the wall: POINT OF CONVERGENCE
screen.desc:
To determine the point of convergence, scientists draw lines through the long axis of the bloodstains.
Click on the highlighted stains to do that.

screen.name: photo3_9
screen.title: Bloodstains on the wall: POINT OF CONVERGENCE
screen.desc:
The computer is going to do the same for a representative sample from the rest of the stains.
Click the button below to start the process.

screen.name: photo3_10
screen.title: Bloodstains on the wall: POINT OF CONVERGENCE
screen.desc: Since the bullet hit the victim’s head, the point of convergence (where the lines meet) indicates where the victim’s head was located during the shooting.
screen.caption: Point of convergence

screen.name: photo3_11
screen.title: Bloodstains on the wall: POINT OF CONVERGENCE
screen.desc:
However, since we are looking at a three-dimensional room, we still need to determine how far away the victim was from the wall.
To do this, we’ll determine the ANGLES OF IMPACT.
screen.caption: Point of convergence

screen.name: photo3_12
screen.title: Bloodstains on the wall: ANGLE OF IMPACT
screen.desc:
Blood spatter patterns typically consist of stains of many different shapes.
Based on these different shapes, scientists can determine the angle in which the blood drops touched the wall.

screen.name: photo3_13
screen.title: Bloodstains on the wall: ANGLE OF IMPACT
screen.desc:
Impact stains have different shapes depending on the ANGLE OF IMPACT.
This angle is formed between the flight path of the blood drop and the target surface the drop strikes.
screen.caption: Position of blood source
Bloodstain
screen.popup: A nearly circular stain is the result of blood travelling from the bleeding person to the wall in a 90° angle.
If the stain is slightly elongated, the blood drop must have touched the wall in an angle less than 90° (here: 40°)
A much more elongated stain is the result of blood striking the wall in a very small angle (here: 10°)

screen.name: photo3_14
screen.title: Bloodstains on the wall: ANGLE OF IMPACT
screen.desc:
Forensic scientists use geometry and trigonometry to calculate the angle of impact for bloodstains.
Click on the highlighted stains to display their angles of impact.

screen.name: photo3_15
screen.title: Bloodstains on the wall: ANGLE OF IMPACT
screen.desc:
The computer is going to calculate the angle of impact for some more representative stains automatically.
Click “Calculate angles” to start the calculations.

screen.name: photo3_16
screen.title: Bloodstains on the wall
screen.desc:
The next step is to transfer this pattern into our 3D blueprint of the crime scene.
The computer will generate the image. Click the button below to start.
screen.caption: Direction of travel
Point of convergence

screen.name: photo3_17
screen.desc: The blueprint indicates where the victim’s head must have been when it was hit by the bullet based on the analysis of the impact stains.
screen.caption: Victim's head

screen.name: photo3_18
screen.desc: Click the bloodstain area on the floor to see if the position of the head is consistent with the drip stains on the floor.
screen.caption: Victim's head
